title: Q3 coverage checkpoint: gap remediation & commit refresh
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Nov 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Q3 coverage checkpoint: gap remediation & commit refresh

Team —

Quick reminder: finance is running the Q3 lock next Friday (11/06). We still have a ~28% gap to plan on ARR-weighted coverage for the quarter and need clean commits for the forecast. Please do the following before EOD Thursday (11/05):

- Reconcile your open deals in the attached spreadsheet (column: rep_commit) with HubSpot.
- Move any late-stage deals that do not have exec alignment or signed next steps to "best case" and reduce committed amounts accordingly.
- Confirm any large SKU/discount exceptions with me and Rishi.

Ben/Laura: flag any deals where we expect a signed order but procurement or security is still outstanding — we can mark those as "pending legal\u002Fprocurement" and separate from commit math.

I've attached a pre-populated sheet (2026_Q3_commit_sheet.xlsx). Use the "rep_updates" tab and then paste the final snapshot link into the HubSpot deal comment. Finance will use that sheet to reconcile.

Thanks — this will be tight but we can get it.

Ava Chen
Senior Sales Operations Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachments: 2026_Q3_commit_sheet.xlsx (spreadsheet)

1) ACME-PRIME (deal ACME-2026-Q3-PRIME): legal requested an additional SOW line for data residency; procurement has signed off on pricing but will not approve PO until SOW is final. I moved the line to "best case" and left a comment with the SOW owner.

Ben Carter

Ben / Ava / Laura —

Thanks for the update. Confirming ACME will not be issuing a PO until the SOW is finalized and countersigned. We expect the SOW review to complete by 11/10 at the earliest. For your reconciliation: please move ACME-PRIME to "best case" for Q3. Finance will plan revenue recognition when an executed SOW and PO are received.

If you need an approval trace, I can provide the procurement ticket ID; ping me and I'll send it over.

— Rebecca (Finance Ops)

title: Semantic fit recap — model picks, prompt sets & drift watch
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Liao <ethan.liao@brightlane.ai>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -0800
Subject: Semantic fit recap — quick model picks and artifacts

Thanks again for your time today, Ethan — appreciated the walkthrough of BrightLane's search use case and the corpus composition (approx. 120k docs, mix of short FAQs and longer reports). Key takeaways + recommended next steps below.

Call highlights
- Primary goal: improve semantic recall for Q&A / search across knowledge base + analyst reports (low tolerance for false positives on legal/regulatory docs).
- Latency constraint: ~150ms p50 target for internal-facing search; throughput ~60 qps at peak.
- Concern: historical drift (vocab changes quarterly), and heterogeneous doc lengths (50 tokens to 8k tokens).

Quick model recommendations (pilot phase)
- Candidate A (higher accuracy, higher cost): redwood-emb-2-large — 1536 dims; strong on semantic grouping and recall@10 in our bench.
- Candidate B (cost-first, smaller footprint): redwood-emb-1-medium — 1024 dims; comparable R@10 on short queries, cheaper for batching.
- Fallback: open-compat-embed-v1 (quantized 768) for long-tail archived docs where latency/cost trumps top-k accuracy.

Bench summary (attached + link)
- Attached: brightlane_embedding_benchmark_v1.csv (MRR, Recall@1/5/10, MAP for each model on your seed prompt set).
- Short summary: redwood-emb-2-large: Recall@10 = 0.86; redwood-emb-1-medium: Recall@10 = 0.78; open-compat-embed-v1: Recall@10 = 0.68.

Prompt / eval artifacts
- Shared prompt sets: prompt_sets_brightlane_v1.md (contains 400 labeled queries, canonical answers, and negative sampling rules). Link in thread: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcD-example-benchmarks
- We recommend keeping the prompt set as the canonical regression suite for model rollouts.

Drift & dimensionality notes
- We saw concept drift in your production sample (monthly cosine mean shift ≈ 0.04) — recommend monthly re-eval and a sentinel set of 200 queries flagged for stability checks.
- Dimensionality tradeoff: 1536 dims gives better cluster separability for longer docs; 1024 dims hits a better cost/throughput point. We'll include both in the pilot to measure incremental improvements vs. cost.

Proposed pilot (50k doc index)
- Timeline: 3 weeks (prep + run): week 1 ingest & indexing, week 2 eval + human review, week 3 fine-tune thresholds/ops.
- Deliverables: benchmark CSV, interactive dashboard, quality regression alerts tuned to your sentinel set, SLO proposal for latency/recall.

Next steps / asks from you
1) Confirm a 1-hour kickoff next week (I can send a calendar invite).
2) Share a sample export (5k docs) + labels for a small subset if available.
3) Procurement contact to loop in for potential PO (cc included).

Attachments: brightlane_embedding_benchmark_v1.csv, prompt_sets_brightlane_v1.md

—
Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0183



From: Ethan Liao <ethan.liao@brightlane.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightLane Procurement <procurement@brightlane.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Nov 2026 09:42:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Semantic fit recap — quick model picks and artifacts

Thanks, Camila — very helpful summary. A couple quick clarifications before we lock the kickoff: 

1) Dimensionality / storage cost: can you ballpark monthly storage (and a rough per-100k-query cost) differential between 1536 vs 1024? We need that for procurement.
2) Drift monitoring: what does the sentinel workflow look like operationally? Who on Redwood manages the alerts vs. who we should own?

We can do a kickoff next Wed 11/10 at 10am PT if that works. Also looping procurement in case we want a short SOW to start the pilot.

—
Ethan Liao
Head of Search, BrightLane Analytics
ethan.liao@brightlane.ai | +1 (617) 444-2290



From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Liao <ethan.liao@brightlane.ai>
Cc: BrightLane Procurement <procurement@brightlane.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Nov 2026 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Semantic fit recap — quick model picks and artifacts

Ethan — great questions, answered inline below and attaching a draft SOW for the pilot (pilot-sow-draft.pdf).

1) Cost/size ballpark (very rough, depends on quantization and caching):
- 1536 dims (redwood-emb-2-large): storage ≈ 230GB per 100k vectors (float32); with 8-bit quantization ~40–60GB. Estimated per-100k-query inference cost (hosted) ≈ $0.35–$0.60 depending on batching and cache hit rate.
- 1024 dims (redwood-emb-1-medium): storage ≈ 150GB per 100k vectors (float32); quantized ~30–45GB. Per-100k-query inference cost ≈ $0.20–$0.40.
Notes: Redwood Optimize will suggest batching/caching knobs and expected unit-cost reductions (we can attach a modeled cost curve once we have query distribution).

2) Drift monitoring & ownership:
- Proposed split: Redwood sets up automated evaluation harness and alerting (monthly job) + dashboard; BrightLane owns the triage workflow and labeling for sentinel queries.
- Alerting thresholds: monthly mean cosine shift > 0.03 or Recall@10 drop > 0.06 triggers an incident.
- We will provision an initial sentinel set of 200 queries and a baseline snapshot; outputs will feed your PagerDuty/Slack channel (we can wire up a webhook).

3) Kickoff: Wed 11/10 10am PT works for our team. I'll send a calendar invite.

Attachments: pilot-sow-draft.pdf

Thanks — I will also loop Rafael and Ben for the technical orchestration and onboarding tasks.

—
Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com

On Tue, Nov 3, 2026 at 09:42 AM Ethan Liao <ethan.liao@brightlane.ai> wrote:
> Thanks, Camila — very helpful summary. A couple quick clarifications before we lock the kickoff: 
> 
> 1) Dimensionality / storage cost: can you ballpark monthly storage (and a rough per-100k-query cost) differential between 1536 vs 1024? We need that for procurement.
> 2) Drift monitoring: what does the sentinel workflow look like operationally? Who on Redwood manages the alerts vs. who we should own?
> 
> We can do a kickoff next Wed 11/10 at 10am PT if that works. Also looping procurement in case we want a short SOW to start the pilot.
> 
> —
> Ethan Liao
> Head of Search, BrightLane Analytics
> ethan.liao@brightlane.ai



From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 4 Nov 2026 09:21:00 -0800
Subject: Internal: BrightLane pilot — ownership + immediate asks

Quick internal note (putting action items here so we have a clean handoff):

Summary: BrightLane accepted the pilot plan and asked for cost ballparks + drift workflow detail. Kickoff: 11/10 10am PT.

Action items
- Rafael: run the eval harness on the 5k sample once Ethan shares it. Can you generate the full bench (MRR/Recall@1/5/10 + cosine shift plots) by EOD Mon 11/8? Attach expected output CSV to the HubSpot record BL-2026-1173.
- Ben: please draft a one-page pricing sketch comparing 1536 vs 1024 options (include quantized storage numbers) — I will send to procurement during kickoff.
- Camila (me): send calendar invite, finalize SOW draft, and own the kickoff agenda.

Threads/links: https://app.redwood.ai/evals/BL-1173/overview — use this to upload results.

Let me know if you want me to loop in Solutions Eng for the kickoff (recommended if Rafael wants help with index tuning).

—
Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com

title: Clarify VAT treatment for platform credit lines (reverse-charge and partial withholding)
preview:
From: Sophie Langston <sophie.langston@paynovo.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: accounts-payable@paynovo.com
Date: Mon, 02 Nov 2026 09:12:00 +0000
Subject: Platform credit on invoice 20261030 — VAT treatment question

Hi Alex,

Thanks for the updated October invoice. Our finance team is holding payment for the credit lines on your invoice dated 2026-10-30 because they need confirmation of two items before we can release funds: 1) whether the platform credit lines are subject to VAT (we typically treat discounts differently), and 2) whether reverse-charge applies for Redwood in our jurisdiction.

Attached is our initial VAT registration extract for PayNovo Ltd (paynovo_vat_certificate.pdf) and a CSV showing how our AP system expects the credit lines mapped (invoice_20261030-platform-credit-lines.csv). Can you confirm the tax treatment for the three credit lines labeled PLATFORM_CREDIT_* and let us know if Redwood will issue a corrected invoice with explicit tax lines, or if we should apply reverse-charge locally?

We need this clarified by COB Wednesday (2026-11-04) so we can include payment in our weekly run. If Redwood requires any additional proof of tax residency or vendor classification, tell us what to upload.

Best,
Sophie Langston
Finance Manager — PayNovo Ltd
Sophie.Langston@paynovo.com

(attachment: paynovo_vat_certificate.pdf - PDF, 232KB)
(attachment: invoice_20261030-platform-credit-lines.csv - CSV, 6KB)

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Langston <sophie.langston@paynovo.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Nov 2026 13:04:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Platform credit on invoice 20261030 — VAT treatment question

Findings:
- Invoice 20261030 shows three credit lines: PLATFORM_CREDIT_TRIAL, PLATFORM_CREDIT_USAGE_ADJ, PLATFORM_CREDIT_PROMO.
- Each credit was entered as an invoice-level adjustment in our ledger (INVOICE_ADJ), not as product-level negative lines. That means the original invoice did not produce distinct VAT line items for each credit.

Recommendation / next steps:

title: Clarify export pathway and pseudonymization artifacts (SSQ)
preview:
From: Ida Jenkins <ida.jenkins@healthlane.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@healthlane.com
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: SSQ follow-up: pseudonymization and export pathway artifacts

Hi Ben / Marissa,

Thanks for the call earlier. A couple of follow-ups from our side as we complete the security questionnaire (SSQ) for our legal and procurement teams:

1) Pseudonymization before export: we need a short description of how PII is pseudonymized prior to any export to downstream partners. Specifically: is the transformation reversible, where are salts/keys stored, and who can access them?

2) Export pathway: please confirm the exact path for exported records destined for our analytics partner (HouseAnalytics). We need the dataflow diagram and an indication of which subprocessors see cleartext/PII vs pseudonymized records.

3) KMS attestation: procurement needs a short attestation that customer salts/KMS keys are under customer or Redwood-managed KMS and the process for key rotation and access logs.

4) Artifacts: can you provide a dataflow diagram and an SSQ spreadsheet with marked answers and references (we prefer an XLSX). If possible include a short timeline for when you can deliver (this week / next week).

Procurement is looping in procurement@healthlane.com for contract timing and DPA annex questions (DPA annex will need a DocuSign).

Attachments: none with this email.

Thanks,
Ida Jenkins
Head of Security & Privacy
HealthLane


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ida Jenkins <ida.jenkins@healthlane.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, procurement@healthlane.com
Date: 2026-11-01T12:10:00-08:00
Subject: Re: SSQ follow-up: pseudonymization and export pathway artifacts

Ida — thanks, good questions. Quick note to confirm ownership and next steps:

- Marissa will prepare the SSQ spreadsheet with explicit answers and references to the artifacts (healthlane_ssq_responses_v1.xlsx).
- Soojin will coordinate with infra on a concise KMS attestation and the export pathway diagram (dataflow_v2.png).
- I will track procurement items (DocuSign DPA annex, redlines) and the schedule.

Proposed delivery: Marissa & Soojin will aim to send the completed SSQ and the dataflow diagram by EOD 2026-11-02. KMS attestation to follow by 2026-11-03. Let me know if you need anything sooner.

Ben
Director, Customer Solutions
Redwood Inference
[attachment: none]


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ida Jenkins <ida.jenkins@healthlane.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, procurement@healthlane.com
Date: 2026-11-02T09:01:00-08:00
Subject: Re: SSQ follow-up: pseudonymization and export pathway artifacts

On Mon, Nov 1, 2026 at 12:10 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Proposed delivery: Marissa & Soojin will aim to send the completed SSQ and the dataflow diagram by EOD 2026-11-02.

Hi Ida — attaching the SSQ answers and the annotated dataflow. High-level summary below; please see the XLSX for cell-level references. [attachment: healthlane_ssq_responses_v1.xlsx] [attachment: dataflow_v2.png]

Summary (answers to your numbered items):

1) Pseudonymization approach:
- We apply a per-tenant non-reversible pseudonymization step for PII fields prior to any export to downstream partners. The transformation is a salted one-way hash (SHA-256 + per-tenant salt) and is intentionally non-reversible.
- Per-tenant salts are stored in Redwood KMS; salts are encrypted with a root KMS key. The salts themselves are treated as secrets and are not exported.
- Access to the salts is tightly controlled by role-based access; only labelled infra/service accounts can perform the hashing operation at export time. Human access to raw salts is restricted and audited.

2) Export pathway and visibility:
- Exports to HouseAnalytics go through Redwood's export pipeline that only contains pseudonymized IDs and the minimal analytic payload (no raw PII). The attached dataflow diagram (dataflow_v2.png) shows this path end-to-end including the export worker, S3 staging, and the HouseAnalytics ingestion endpoint.
- Subprocessors that process pseudonymized exports: S3 (storage), CDN (transfer), HouseAnalytics (ingestion). None of these subprocessors receive raw PII when the pipeline is configured for pseudonymization.

3) KMS attestation / key custody:
- Redwood operates a KMS cluster; for customers that request dedicated key custody we support customer-managed keys (CMKs) via bring-your-own-key (BYOK) integrations.
- We will provide a short KMS attestation describing key custody model, rotation frequency (90 days for root wrapping keys), and how access logs are stored. Soojin is preparing that now.

4) Telemetry and retention:
- Application-level telemetry (request metadata, latency, error codes) is retained for 30 days by default. Telemetry does not contain raw PII fields; identifiers are pseudonymized as described above.

5) Additional options / controls:
- If HealthLane requires salt escrow / additional separation, we can explore a scoped BYOK workflow or an on-prem data export adapter.

Next steps:
- Please confirm whether the per-tenant non-reversible hashing meets your pseudonymization requirement, and whether HealthLane needs escrowed salts or a BYOK (customer-managed KMS) option.
- If procurement needs the DPA annex signed before we proceed with BYOK, we can prioritize that path.

Regards,
Marissa Cole
Senior Privacy & Compliance Manager
Redwood Inference



From: Ida Jenkins <ida.jenkins@healthlane.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: HealthLane Procurement <procurement@healthlane.com>
Date: 2026-11-03T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: SSQ follow-up: pseudonymization and export pathway artifacts

- SOC 2 pointer: can you provide a link or short reference to the specific SOC 2 report we should cite (redacted where necessary)?
- DPA annex: procurement is ready to accept DocuSign; please send the DPA annex link and we will route for signature.

looping in procurement for timeline and contracting: procurement@healthlane.com

Ida

title: MSA redline: compressed calendar window — alternate plan
preview:
From: Jordan Miles <j.miles@acmehealth.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: legal@acmehealth.com
Date: Sun, 1 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MSA redline cadence

Camila —

Thanks for the draft and the suggested timeline. We reviewed redline_v3_acme.pdf overnight and legal is asking for a consolidated 90‑minute session to run through the remaining items rather than multiple short touchpoints. Our pull for next week is tight: the only contiguous block that works for the cross‑functional team (legal + procurement + engineering) is Wednesday 11/4 between 13:00–14:30 PT.

If Redwood can do that, we can close the remaining outstanding points and push DocuSign the same day. If not, we need an escalation plan in place — we cannot stretch this beyond Friday for budget cycle reasons.

Jordan
Jordan Miles

title: POC alignment: measurable SLAs, token economics & ops sprint
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Tayla Reynolds <tayla@nextoptics.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: POC alignment: measurable SLAs, token economics & ops sprint

Tayla — great to meet today. Short recap and what we'll deliver next: 

Call highlights / agreed scope
- 2-week POC (sandbox) that mirrors a production inference path for the Summarization flow we reviewed.
- Run two load profiles: interactive (bursty traffic) and steady-state throughput.

Success criteria (quantitative):
- Latency: P95 <= 250ms for 512-token inference (measured at our edge ingress)
- Throughput: sustain 100 QPS (concurrent user mix) with tail latency within SLOs
- Cost: amortized inference cost <= $0.004 per 1k tokens for the POC profile (we'll show cost breakdown by model/quantization)
- Quality: 200 human-rated prompts, >= 80% rated "acceptable" (binary useful/not useful), and no more than 5% factuality failures on extraction tasks
- Reliability: error rate (5xx/timeout) < 1% during test window

Instrumentation & deliverables from Redwood
- Dedicated sandbox instance (private tenant) + shared test harness and sample payloads (attached: poc-runbook-v1.pdf, nextoptics-sample-payload.json)
- Perf instrumentation: request-level traces, token-level cost breakdown, and a pre-built script to compute P50/P95 and error rates
- Human-eval playbook: sampling plan + evaluation form for NextOptics raters

Next steps / timeline
1) We provision the sandbox and share access (target: 2026-11-03)
2) Kickoff workshop (tech + security) — proposed 2026-11-04 10:00 PT
3) Run load tests + human eval in week of 2026-11-09; closeout readout 2026-11-16

Owners
- Redwood AE: Ben Carter (point)
- Security & compliance: Marissa Cole (SOC2 audit artifacts, audit logs)
- Infra & performance: Karthik Iyer (load harness + autoscaling config)

Any additional asks from your side? You mentioned procurement contact and SOC2 — please loop them in so we can share the evidence bundle.

Attachments referenced: poc-runbook-v1.pdf (runbook, test profiles), nextoptics-sample-payload.json (sample corpus)

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0192

From: Tayla Reynolds <tayla@nextoptics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, procurement@nextoptics.com
Date: 2026-11-01T11:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: POC alignment: measurable SLAs, token economics & ops sprint

Thanks Ben — this looks good and captures the discussion. A few clarifications from our side: 

1) Data residency / logs: NextOptics requires that the sandbox data be hosted in the US region and that per-request audit logs are retained for 90 days. Can you confirm the sandbox will meet that?
2) Security evidence: we need SOC2 Type II report and a list of encryption-at-rest KMS options (customer-managed preferred). Marissa, could you flag items we should expect to hand over?
3) Human eval panel: can Redwood support initial annotation tooling, or do you expect us to provide raters and tooling? If you can provide a lightweight UI that saves rater results to a CSV that would speed ramp.
4) Cost target: the $0.004/1k tokens number sounds good for the POC window; please include a sample breakdown for model variant A vs A-quantized.

Procurement: procurement@nextoptics.com is on copy. We usually run a short one-page MSA for trials — procurement will ping you with the standard PO flow.

I'll confirm the kickoff time after I check internal calendars.

— Tayla
VP Product, NextOptics
Tayla.Reynolds@nextoptics.com
Attachment: nextoptics-data-schema.json (sample schema for evaluation payload)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Tayla Reynolds <tayla@nextoptics.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, procurement@nextoptics.com
Date: 2026-11-02T08:20:00-08:00
Subject: Re: POC alignment: measurable SLAs, token economics & ops sprint

Tayla — quick answers and action items: 

- Data residency / logs: confirmed — sandbox will be provisioned in the US region and we will enable per-request audit logs with 90-day retention. Marissa will share the evidence bundle and KMS integration options (including CMK/HSM notes).
- Human eval: Redwood can provide the lightweight annotation UI (exports CSV/JSON). We'll ship a preconfigured instance and a sample evaluation sheet.
- Cost breakdown: attaching a short test-plan spreadsheet (poc-test-plan.xlsx) that shows token counts, model choices (base, 8-bit quant), and estimated cost per 1k tokens under POC workloads.

Proposed internal owner assignments (confirming):
- Marissa: security evidence & audit artifacts (SOC2 package, KMS options), due 2026-11-03 EOD
- Karthik: load harness + autoscale policy + perf scripting, deliverable 2026-11-03 EOD
- Ben: coordinate kickoff, test windows, and readout (schedule invite 2026-11-04 10:00 PT)

Operational notes on metrics collection: 
- Latency measurement: compute P50/P95/P99 at service ingress, and also report token-level time to first byte for sampling (helps isolate model vs infra).
- Throughput: scripted linear ramp to 100 QPS with 5-minute hold; we will also run a burst test at 250 concurrent requests to validate graceful degradation and fallback routing.
- Quality: we will run blind human evals on 200 sampled prompts and capture both binary useful flag and a 3-point severity for factual errors. We'll provide csv of results and a short summary dashboard.

Ben — I'll send the kickoff invite once Tayla confirms. Marissa / Karthik — please confirm you can hit the 11/03 EOD internal deliverables so we hit the kickoff date.

Thanks,
Ben

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-03T14:05:00-08:00
Subject: INTERNAL: NextOptics POC prep checklist (owners & quick wins)

Internal note — short checklist before kickoff: 

Marissa (security/compliance)
- Share SOC2 Type II PDF + control matrix (task) — target 11/03 EOD
- Prepare KMS options doc (customer-managed key flow + rotation notes)
- Verify audit log retention config for the sandbox (90 days) and ACLs for NextOptics access

Karthik (infra/perf)
- Stand up sandbox tenant in US region; enable per-request tracing and token-cost headers
- Load harness: provide ramp script and autoscale knobs; smoke run by 11/03 EOD
- Prepare a short runbook entry for fallback behavior at 250 concurrent requests

Ben (engagement)
- Send kickoff invite to Tayla + procurement + internal owners (scheduled 11/04 10:00 PT)
- Share poc-runbook-v1.pdf and poc-test-plan.xlsx to the shared Drive link and HubSpot record HS-5642

Risks / escalations to note
- If human-eval sampling is below 200 unique prompts we need to extend the window (impacting readout date)
- If initial perf shows P95 > 300ms we will toggle model quantization and re-run; flag to customer as "performance remediation" step

I'll sync with both of you 30m before kickoff for a last-minute health check.

— Ben
Account Executive

title: Invoice 2026-11-01 | PO mismatch on PO 4231 — conditional wire option
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: Teri Nguyen <teri.nguyen@solidwave.com>, SolidWave AP <ap@solidwave.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Invoice 2026-11-01 | PO mismatch on PO 4231 — conditional wire option

Hi Teri / SolidWave AP team,

We posted Invoice #2026-11-01 (attached) for the October usage period. Our collections queue shows the invoice as due, but AP flagged a PO mismatch: your PO on file is 4231 while the PO number printed on the invoice is 4321. Can you confirm which PO your AP intends to apply? We want to avoid misapplied payments.

If PO 4231 is correct, we can reissue a corrected invoice immediately. To help unblock payment, we're also prepared to offer a conditional same‑day wire: if you confirm PO 4231 and send the vendor onboarding packet (vendor name and tax/VAT cert) to ap@solidwave.com, our payments team can release a wire once they receive AP authorization. Options:
- Confirm PO 4231 and provide vendor onboarding/tax doc -> we reissue invoice + expedited wire (same business day)
- If onboarding will take longer, we can accept ACH within 5 business days or virtual card (we can discuss fees)

Attachments: Redwood_Invoice_2026-11-01.pdf (current), SolidWave_PO_4231.pdf (PO we have on record).

Please let me know the fastest path for your AP. If you prefer, I can loop in Ben from Payments to share exact wire instructions and a wire authorization form.

Thanks,
Avery
Avery Johnson | Senior Account Manager
Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0182



From: Teri Nguyen <teri.nguyen@solidwave.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, SolidWave AP <ap@solidwave.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Invoice 2026-11-01 | PO mismatch on PO 4231 — conditional wire option

Avery — thanks for the heads up. Quick update from Treasury: PO 4231 is the correct PO on their side. The reason payment is on hold is vendor onboarding — Finance needs an updated vendor W-9 (or EU VAT cert for our EU entity) plus a signed vendor agreement before they will release wire.

I attached the vendor onboarding checklist they sent. AP can move quickly once the W-9 / tax residency is on file. Can Redwood provide a completed vendor form or the tax docs? AP prefers a one‑page vendor letter or an on‑file W-9.

If you can provide those, I’ll escalate to treasury to consider a same‑day wire. Otherwise we’ll process as ACH on the next run.

Thanks — Teri
Teri Nguyen
Senior Treasury Analyst, SolidWave Inc.
teri.nguyen@solidwave.com

On Mon, Nov 1, 2026 at 09:12 AM Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com> wrote:
> We posted Invoice #2026-11-01 (attached) ...


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Teri Nguyen <teri.nguyen@solidwave.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T11:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Invoice 2026-11-01 | PO mismatch on PO 4231 — conditional wire option

Looping finance & payments to propose stopgap options so we don’t slip into overdue status. Suggested playbook:

1) Provide Redwood's vendor tax docs (attach W-9) and a short vendor letter confirming legal name and bank account. That should satisfy their onboarding step.
2) If SolidWave treasury will not approve without a signed agreement, offer a conditional wire: payments release funds to Redwood on receipt of AP's wire authorization email and vendor onboarding docs; Redwood confirms receipt and issues credit memo if needed after corrected invoice is posted.
3) If they cannot wire, offer virtual card as a fallback (we can absorb a portion of the processing fee for this invoice if needed).

Ben — can you prepare a wire authorization template and our W-9 so Avery can push to Teri ASAP? Laura — flag priority on the invoice reissue if they confirm PO 4231.

Marissa
Director, Revenue Operations

title: Dedicated capacity validation — performance next steps and test plan
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@nexahealth.com>; Sarah Wu <sarah.wu@nexahealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-01T15:12:00-08:00
Subject: Post-demo recap — perf targets and validation plan

Thanks again for carving out time today — great session. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment on SLOs and the validation plan if we move to a Dedicated (reserved) pool: 

Key SLO targets you asked about (NexaHealth goals):
- Latency: p95 end-to-end generation <= 180 ms for 256-token streaming completions (single-turn predictive API)
- Sustained throughput: 5k tokens/sec aggregate across a pool serving 1k concurrent lightweight streams
- Concurrency: steady-state 800 concurrent live sessions with occasional 1k user spikes (burst window ~3m)
- Streaming behavior: partial tokens should appear within 60 ms after model emits them (low first-byte latency)

Initial sizing hypothesis (ballpark):
- Hardware shortlist: H100 (80GB int8 path), A100 (40/80GB), and one inference-optimized L4-based node for lower-cost baseline testing.
- Reserved throughput target: configure pool for 6k tokens/sec headroom; autoscale policy to add bursts up to 9k tokens/sec for up to 10 minutes.
- Suggested concurrency shards: 4 nodes with continuous batching + prefix/KV cache to keep p95 in budget; we'll validate with synthetic streams.

Attached: nexa_slo_estimates_v1.xlsx (rough compute-time and token-cost model), plus demo-recording-20261101.mp4.

Proposed validation plan (short):
1) We run 3-day perf run: baseline (A100), H100, and L4 variant. Each run: 6h warmup, 12h steady state with realistic mix (60% short chat, 30% multi-turn, 10% long-context batch queries).
2) Metrics: p50/p90/p95 latency, tail latency at 99.9, token throughput, GPU utilization, cost per 1M tokens, and streaming first-byte latency.
3) Failure modes: run a few overloaded scenarios to confirm graceful fallback behavior (route to cheaper variant or reduce batch size).
4) Deliverable: a one-page recommendation with cost/latency tradeoffs + runbook for Reserved Pool (including autoscale knobs and rollback thresholds).

Ask from Nexa: can you confirm the expected traffic mix and whether any sessions require sub-100ms first-byte guarantees? Also share a sample 1-hour replay or synthetic script if available so we can align the test profile.

I'll loop in Engineering (Marcus/Vanessa) to sketch the test matrix and infra needs. If procurement wants a provisional pricing model we can provide an initial reserved-capacity quote after the first perf run.

Thanks,
Alex Martinez
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0142

[Attachment: nexa_slo_estimates_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet)]
[Attachment: demo-recording-20261101.mp4 (video)]

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@nexahealth.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T17:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap — perf targets and validation plan

Thanks Alex — clear summary. A couple of engineering clarifications from my side before we propose the final runbook: 

1) Tokenization and sequence lengths: the cost model in the spreadsheet assumes average output length 256 tokens and average input length 32 tokens. If Nexa's multi-turn sessions tend to have longer context (~1k tokens), GPU memory and KV cache behavior will change the sizing significantly.

2) Streaming-first-byte: to hit sub-60ms we need to ensure the serving path uses small-batch low-latency kernels (no heavy quantization path that batches aggressively). H100 gives a better tail profile for streaming, A100 is closer on cost per token for large batches. L4 is cost-efficient but we should expect higher p95 for streaming workloads.

3) Autoscale policy: propose target utilization 65% for each reserved node and burst policy to add two hot nodes at 85% sustained for 3m. That gives room for concurrency spikes while keeping steady-state cost predictable.

I'll draft a test matrix matrix.csv and suggested JMeter/streaming-playback commands for the synthetic run. Vanessa — can you capture the exact req/resp shapes from the demo (sample payloads) so we can mimic client behavior (partial chunk sizes, keepalive)?

-Marcus
Principal Performance Engineer | Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.ai


From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@nexahealth.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sarah Wu <sarah.wu@nexahealth.com>, procurement@nexahealth.com
Date: 2026-11-02T09:18:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap — perf targets and validation plan

Alex / Marcus — appreciate the detailed plan. Quick answers from our side: 

- Traffic mix: the 60/30/10 split is accurate for most hours. Peak hours during business ops see closer to 70% short chat. The multi-turn sessions often accumulate to ~600–900 tokens over a typical support case, not 1k typically.
- First-byte: ideally <80ms. We can accept 120–180ms p95 for whole-turn latency as long as the streaming chunks begin earlier; i.e., users see incremental text quickly.
- We have a 1-hour replay of anonymized events we can share (S3 link); I'll coordinate with Sarah to get you upload access.

Procurement note: we need the provisional TCO before our Nov 15 budget review (ballpark cost + one-time validation engagement fee).

We'll get back with the S3 link today. Thanks,
Liam
Head of Platform Integrations | NexaHealth
liam.oconnor@nexahealth.com


From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@nexahealth.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-02T10:32:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap — perf targets and validation plan

Liam — thanks, that clarifies a lot. A couple of operational asks so our perf runs are realistic: 

- Please confirm the TLS termination pattern (client-side keepalive, connection reuse counts) and whether users typically open a long-lived stream or re-open per-turn.
- For the replay: we need per-event timestamps and an indication of which sessions include file attachments (those increase token counts). If you can't provide raw timestamps, a synthetic distribution (events/minute) is fine.

Next steps on our side: I'll stand up the three candidate pools (H100, A100, L4) in our staging region (us-west-2) and schedule the first A100 baseline run for 2026-11-04 01:00 UTC. Marcus — I'll share the test harness and a small checklist for pre-run validation.

-Vanessa
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference
vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai

title: Monthly KPI packet + QBR prep — exec notes
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Brooks <tbrooks@cloudport.io>
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Monthly KPI packet + QBR prep — exec notes

Team — attaching the November KPI packet and a short agenda for next week's QBR prep (attached: 20261101-kpi-dashboard.pdf, qbr-prep-agenda.gdoc). High-level callouts from the dashboard (please scan before EOD Monday):

- Latency: P95 increased ~18% week-over-week driven by long-tail spikes on /chat-route (seen during the 10/24 deploy). Rolling average still within SLO but the tail risks affecting latency SLAs for Dedicated customers.
- Cost: Token spend is on a +14% trajectory month-over-month; model mix shift and a few high-throughput accounts are the main contributors. See cost-breakdown-nov.csv for per-customer run-rates.
- Uptime: 99.93% last 30d. Two short infra incidents (2–3 min each) caused minor error bumps; full RCA in the doc.
- Usage: Active routes up 9%, peak qps increased ~12% at 9:30–11:30 PT on weekdays.

Asks for the QBR / OKR check-in:
1) Marcus — can you confirm the proposed mitigation (cache TTL bump + guardrail for the /chat-route) and whether we can ship a hotfix this week?
2) Rishi — please prepare the one-slide cost breakdown for execs (top 3 drivers + 90-day run-rate).
3) Laura — approval to include a short customer-impact blurb for Brightline Health in the executive summary? Elena is copied.
4) Grace — add a short section on Ops readiness and staffing gaps for next quarter.

Please comment inline on the gdoc if you have slide edits. If no major objections, I’ll finalize the QBR deck Tuesday morning and circulate the exec summary.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Head of Product
Redwood Inference

Attachments: 20261101-kpi-dashboard.pdf (18 pages, charts + highlights), qbr-prep-agenda.gdoc (live), cost-breakdown-nov.csv (per-account run rates)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Tom Brooks <tbrooks@cloudport.io>
Date: 2026-11-01T13:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Monthly KPI packet + QBR prep — exec notes

Thanks Ava — quick notes inline.

On Mon, Nov 1, 2026 at 9:12 AM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Team — attaching the November KPI packet and a short agenda for next week's QBR prep...

- Latency: Confirmed that the 10/24 deploy introduced a regression in context handling for the /chat-route which created long tail. Ops rolled back the offending kernel within 40m; we still had cache warming issues afterward.
- Mitigation: I support the cache TTL bump for public routes (suggest +25% for route group A) plus a short-term circuit (reject > X tokens from unverified origins). Ops can ship hotfix Wed EOD if we greenlight.
- Cost: I dug into the cost-breakdown CSV — two enterprise accounts (Brightline and one unnamed) are ~46% of incremental spend. We should propose a pricing/usage review with Brightline in the QBR.

Action items I can take this week:
- Draft the technical appendix slide showing before/after P95 for the hotfix (I'll attach a small chart).
- Coordinate with SRE to get the hotfix milestone on Wednesday.

– Marcus
Senior Engineering Manager, Serving Runtime

Team — attaching the November KPI packet and a short agenda for next week's QBR prep (attached: 20261101-kpi-dashboard.pdf, qbr-prep-agenda.gdoc). High-level callouts from the dashboard (please scan before EOD Monday):

- Latency: P95 increased ~18% week-over-week driven by long-tail spikes on /chat-route (seen during the 10/24 deploy). Rolling average still within SLO but the tail risks affecting latency SLAs for Dedicated customers.
- Cost: Token spend is on a +14% trajectory month-over-month; model mix shift and a few high-throughput accounts are the main contributors. See cost-breakdown-nov.csv for per-customer run-rates.
- Uptime: 99.93% last 30d. Two short infra incidents (2–3 min each) caused minor error bumps; full RCA in the doc.
- Usage: Active routes up 9%, peak qps increased ~12% at 9:30–11:30 PT on weekdays.

Asks for the QBR / OKR check-in:
1) Marcus — can you confirm the proposed mitigation (cache TTL bump + guardrail for the /chat-route) and whether we can ship a hotfix this week?
2) Rishi — please prepare the one-slide cost breakdown for execs (top 3 drivers + 90-day run-rate).
3) Laura — approval to include a short customer-impact blurb for Brightline Health in the executive summary? Elena is copied.
4) Grace — add a short section on Ops readiness and staffing gaps for next quarter.

Please comment inline on the gdoc if you have slide edits. If no major objections, I’ll finalize the QBR deck Tuesday morning and circulate the exec summary.

title: Secure hosting discovery recap & next steps
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Dr. Leila Hassan <leila.hassan@medivar.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -0700
Subject: Secure hosting discovery recap & next steps

Leila — thanks again for taking time today. Below is a concise recap from our call and proposed next steps so your infra and security teams have a clear path forward. If anything below is inaccurate or missing, please flag and I’ll update.

High-level summary (pain & goals)
- Primary pain: must run PHI workloads on customer-controlled infrastructure with strict auditability and no uncontrolled egress.
- Goal: evaluate Redwood Private (VPC/on‑prem) for LLM-powered clinical summarization (pilot ~Q1 2027) with clear cryptographic controls and SOC2-level evidence.

Current stack & constraints noted
- On‑prem Kubernetes cluster (Rancher-managed) hosting FHIR store and ETL pipelines.
- Storage: Ceph/S3-compatible object store; nightly backups to internal tape archive.
- Network: extremely limited outbound — only allowed egress to vendor APIs on allowlist; strict firewall rules; NAT only via approved jump hosts.
- Identity: Okta SSO in place; SCIM provisioning; RBAC enforced.
- Compliance: must maintain auditable key rotation and BYOK/HSM integration for KMS; procurement will require a completed security questionnaire and SOC2 artifacts.

Success criteria for pilot
- Data never leaves customer-controlled network except to allowlisted endpoints agreed in advance.
- Encryption at rest using customer-managed keys (BYOK) with HSM-backed KMS.
- Traceable audit logs for inference requests and model versioning retained for 12 months.
- Performance: acceptable median latency for 512-token prompts (<300ms median token generation locally is preferred; we can set SLOs during pilot).

Open technical risks we flagged
- Air‑gap readiness: if their environment is fully air‑gapped, we’ll need an offline deployment path and signed artifact transfer.
- Egress policy: Redwood’s observability relies on throwing telemetry to a collector — need to confirm whether a pull model or brokered push to their SIEM is acceptable.
- KMS integration: confirm HSM vendor + supported APIs (PKCS#11, CloudHSM, etc.).

Immediate next steps (owner & ETA)
1) Aditya: share NDA + an initial private deployment checklist and our SOC2 summary PDF — by EOD 2026-11-02. (attached)
2) Leila / Medivar: provide a redacted network diagram and list of allowed external FQDNs/ips for the pilot — by 2026-11-06. (link below)
3) Ava: schedule a 60min technical workshop with Medivar infra + security to walk deployment topology and KMS flow — target week of 2026-11-09.
4) Grace: prepare a high-level pricing sketch for a 3-month pilot and a path to Dedicated capacity — by 2026-11-12.

title: SLA baseline revision request — availability credit & latency SLOs
preview:
From: Maya Collins <maya.collins@mw-inc.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Oliver Park <oliver.park@mw-inc.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Oct 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request to revisit SLA baseline and monthly credit cap

Ben —

Thanks for the demo earlier. MW Inc. is moving rapidly on production timelines and our procurement team flagged two items in Redwood's standard SLA that we need to reconcile before signature:

1) Baseline uptime: Currently the standard contract reads 99.95% as the availability target. Given our regulatory uptime expectations for the Tier-1 checkout flow, we need to discuss whether a 99.9% baseline is acceptable for our use case, or whether Redwood can offer additional credits for the higher bar.

2) Monthly credit cap: We understand commercial constraints, but a hard cap on credits means our remediation economics are exposed in a worse-case outage. We'd like to propose either raising the monthly credit cap or adjusting the credit tiers to be more linear for outage bands in the 0.5%–1.5% delta.

We attached MW's standard addendum with typical language and examples of how our finance team models credits. Can you let us know who at Redwood should handle the legal/procurement redline? We're aiming to close next week.

—Maya

[Attachment: MW_SLA_Addendum_Template.docx (docx)]


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Collins <maya.collins@mw-inc.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 30 Oct 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request to revisit SLA baseline and monthly credit cap

Hi Maya —

Really appreciate the clear ask and the timeline. Short answers up front: we can be flexible, but need to balance availability guarantees with predictable programmatic limits for all customers. Proposed next steps:

- I’ll loop in our commercial lead (Karthik) and our legal ops (Laura) to run a targeted redline.
- Can you confirm the specific traffic and p95/p99 latency SLOs for the checkout flow? That helps us size the guardrails (e.g., dedicated pools vs hosted fallbacks).
- We’ll push a counter-proposal within 48 business hours that addresses both a 99.9% baseline option and an alternative credit schedule (non-linear bands).

Folks: Maya is procurement lead at MW. They’ve attached their addendum — noted ask is to either accept 99.9% baseline or adjust credit tiers/cap. I think a reasonable compromise is: 99.9% baseline with a higher credit multiplier only for the first two months of production, reverting to our standard cap thereafter. Karthik/Laura, can you take the lead on the redline?

Maya — expect a revised SLA from us by Tuesday EOD.

Best,
Ben
Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference

[Attachment referenced: redwood_sla_proposal_v2.pdf (pdf)]


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Nov 2026 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: MW Inc. SLA redline strategy

Team —

I reviewed MW's addendum and sketched two options for negotiation (attached):

Option A — Production ramp accommodation
- Offer 99.9% baseline for first 60 days after go-live.
- Monthly credit multiplier = 1.5x for months 1–2, capped at 20% of monthly committed spend.
- After 60 days revert to standard 99.95% baseline and standard cap.

Option B — Higher baseline, adjusted latency clause
- Commit to 99.95% baseline (no temporary change) but add an explicit latency SLO for checkout: p95 <= 120ms, p99 <= 300ms with a small credit band tied to sustained latency deviation.
- Raise monthly credit cap by +10% for enterprise customers with dedicated pools.

Recommendation: start with Option A as it shows customer flexibility for ramp without changing permanent template. Use a 60-day pilot concession and include an automatic review clause.

Laura, can you draft the redline (track changes) based on Option A. Ben — can you confirm MW's committed monthly spend so we can calibrate the cap percent?

Thanks,
Karthik
Senior Sales, Enterprise

[Attachment: MW-SLA-redline-v1.docx (docx)]


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Nov 2026 15:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Internal: MW Inc. SLA redline strategy

Ben/Karthik —

Draft redline attached. I incorporated Option A language but added a clause to limit the temporary multiplier to incidents impacting the production checkout namespace only (so internal non-core incidents don't trigger the elevated multiplier). I also added an explicit termination clause if customer attempts to re-request the concession after the 60-day window.

Ben — please confirm committed monthly spend. Once confirmed I'll export a clean PDF and send to Maya for review.

Laura Bennett
Legal Ops, Redwood Inference

[Attachment: MW-SLA-redline-v1.docx (docx)]

title: ArborAI MAP kickoff — stakeholder alignment & 8-week evaluation plan
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Lena Ortiz <lena.ortiz@arborai.com>, Tom Nguyen <tom.nguyen@arborai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 1 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: ArborAI MAP kickoff — stakeholder alignment & 8-week evaluation plan

Hi Lena / Tom,

Thanks again for the demo last week — excited to be working with ArborAI. I wanted to kick off a short Mutual Action Plan (MAP) so we can align on goals, stakeholders, and a realistic close window. My suggested high-level plan: 

- Week 0 (Kickoff): scope, roles, quick access to test data/environment
- Weeks 1–2: rapid integration & smoke tests (SE support)
- Weeks 3–4: performance validation & cost analysis (sample workloads)
- Weeks 5–6: security & compliance review + procurement prep
- Week 7–8: pilot wrap, decision point / PO or next steps

Primary objectives we heard: verify latency under peak load, validate embedding quality on your search pipeline, and confirm operational costs at scale. Can you confirm the internal stakeholders you want looped in for each milestone (e.g., security, infra, procurement)? Also please confirm if an 8-week window looks feasible from your side.

I attached a blank MAP template to make this easy to populate; happy to run the kickoff meeting and update the doc live. Proposed kickoff slots next week: Tue 11/3 10–11am PT or Wed 11/4 2–3pm PT — what works best for your team?

Attaching: ArborAI_MAP_Draft_v1.xlsx (MAP template)

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0172

--

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Request: additional eval credits for pilot

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the help during our trial. We're in the middle of an internal legal review and multiple people on our engineering and ML teams are trying to finish integration tests. Several of our engineers have hit the credit cap for isolated pilots and are asking for short-term top-ups so we can complete a consolidated evaluation next week.

Can Redwood issue another set of evaluation credits or extend the trial window for ScaleBridge (account id SB-993)? We've had people request credits under different emails (I can list them) because teams were testing isolated flows. We want to avoid losing momentum.

If there are any compliance checks you need from our side, let me know — happy to provide a point of contact.

Best,
Eli Torres
Head of ML
ScaleBridge

Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: Request: additional eval credits for pilot

Hi Eli —

Appreciate the context. To move quickly I need a short list of the accounts/emails that requested credits (and whether they belong to ScaleBridge employees). Also can you confirm whether any of these were invited by contractors or external partners? We do occasional one-time top-ups for coordinated pilot teams but we need to validate against our abuse patterns first.

Jordan, looping you in to see if there are open deals or recent bulk signups for SB-993.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com

Date: 2026-11-01T10:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: additional eval credits for pilot

Ava —

Thanks. List below — all are employees at ScaleBridge:
- eli@scalebridge.com
- olivia.park@scalebridge.com
- samir.khan@scalebridge.com
- tegan.li@scalebridge.com
- infra@scalebridge.com (shared service account)

Samir and Tegan started separate small pilots but are on the same engineering org. infra@ is our automation account used for CI replay jobs. We only want a short, one-week extension or a one-time credit grant to finish integration validation.

Happy to sign an addendum if required.

Best,
Eli

---


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T15:05:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Request: additional eval credits for pilot — suspected credential reuse?

Marissa — forwarding ScaleBridge thread. At first glance this looks like a normal pilot request, except our request history shows a cluster of credit top-ups tied to the same billing fingerprint but coming from 5 distinct emails over the last 10 days. I attached a quick snapshot.

Observations:
- Multiple extra requests from accounts that map to SB-993 but with overlapping IPs/UA strings (attached).
- infra@scalebridge.com is used for CI and shows repeated automated sessions that consumed tokens overnight.
- No prior exemption on file for bulk/team grants under this account.

Options:
1) Approve a one-time, limited credit grant (e.g., 20k tokens) after manual verification and require a signed pilot addendum.
2) Deny and offer a paid short-term pilot (discounted) and help them migrate the infra runs to a paid org quota.

We need fraud/abuse perspective before we issue more free credits.

--
Jordan Blake
Sales | Redwood Inference
Attachment: scalebridge_request_history.csv; scalebridge_user_agent_snapshot.txt


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-02T10:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: additional eval credits for pilot — suspected credential reuse?

Team — thanks for the signals and the quick triage. Given the overlapping fingerprints and the infra account behaviour, I want to be conservative here: we should not blanket-issue additional complimentary credits without tighter controls.

Action items (owner in parens):
- Send a templated response to ScaleBridge offering a one-week limited credit grant (20k tokens) conditioned on: 1) a signed pilot addendum; 2) confirmation that infra@ is a company-managed service account and not a recycled/shared credential; 3) a list of employee IDs for the five requesters (Ava).
- Marissa: please log the file in abuse-tracker and mark as monitored; include the UA/IP snapshot in the incident.
- Jordan: prepare a commercial alternative (two-week discounted pilot price) to present if they decline the addendum.

We also need to update the deal record (HB-4582) with this decision and the attachments. If they provide the requested confirmation and addendum, we can approve the one-time credit within 24 hours. If anything on their verification looks inconsistent, deny and route them to paid pilot.

I'll take ownership of any escalations from legal.

Rishi Malhotra
Director, Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
rishi.malhotra@redwood.com

title: Ingress conduit options — VPC peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@harbortech.com>
To: Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Oct 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Ingress conduit options — shorthand question

Hi Tessa,

Attaching a quick topology (HarborTech_network_topology.pdf) — were evaluating private connectivity for a Redwood Private deployment and want a short recommendation on the right ingress conduit. high-level constraints:
- No traffic should traverse the public Internet (regulatory requirement).
- Cross-account access (Redwood managed account <-> our prod VPC).
- Expected sustained traffic: 150-250 Mbps, occasional spikes to ~600 Mbps.
- 99th percentile latency goal to Redwood inference endpoints < 15 ms (us-east-1).
- We want minimal operational surface but strong isolation and auditability for SOC2 (in progress).

Options on the table: VPC peering, AWS PrivateLink (VPC Endpoint Services), or a site-to-site VPN (IPSec) to our on-prem or Transit Gateway.

Can you call out the pragmatic tradeoffs (security/compliance, maintenance, failover, throughput, cost) and which you would pilot first for HarborTech? Also: who typically terminates TLS and where should KMS live for Private deployments?

Thanks — if this is better as a quick call we can set one up.

Liam O'Connor
Network Architect, HarborTech
liam.oconnor@harbortech.com
+1 (415) 555-0147

Attachments: HarborTech_network_topology.pdf (diagram), sample firewall rules (referenced)


From: Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@harbortech.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Oct 2026 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Ingress conduit options — shorthand question

Thanks for the diagram, Liam  

Quick summary up front: for your constraints Id recommend piloting PrivateLink (interface endpoints) first with a fallback path (Transit Gateway + peering) for high-throughput batch traffic. Rationale in bullets below.

Why PrivateLink first (short):
- Strong isolation: connections are per-service and dont expose your VPC routes to Redwoods account (reduced blast radius).
- Compliance-friendly: AWS flow logs + VPC endpoint-level logging + easy isolation for audits.
- Operational simplicity for application-level calls (mutual TLS between your services and Redwood).

Caveats:
- Throughput per ENI can be constrained; we treat heavy sustained bandwidth with either multiple endpoints or an alternate peering/transit route.
- Slightly higher per-GB cost vs peering, and additional ENI management.

When peering or VPN makes sense:
- VPC Peering (or Transit Gateway attachments) for sustained, high-throughput low-latency traffic where cross-account route exposure is acceptable and you control both VPCs.
- Site-to-site VPN: good as a temporary fallback or for on-prem connectivity; increased latency and head-of-line encryption cost; maintenance overhead (rekeying, tunnels).

TLS/KMS:
- We recommend mutual TLS terminated at the Redwood Private control plane for session management, with customer-side TLS for end-to-end encryption where you require key ownership.
- For regulated customers wanting exclusive key control, we support customer-managed KMS/HSM (you supply the CMK) in Private deployments; otherwise Redwood-managed KMS with strict audit logs is common.

Next steps I propose:
1) Quick 30-min technical sync to look at traffic profile and failure modes (please share p95/p99 request sizes and concurrency).
2) Pilot with a single PrivateLink endpoint in us-east-1 for control-plane calls + a small production route through a Transit Gateway attachment for higher-throughput workloads.
3) We'll supply a short checklist (redwood_privatedeploy_checklist.xlsx) outlining required firewall rules, CIDR ranges, and audit hooks.

Ben and Kimberly are looped in — Ben can sketch the comparison matrix and Kimberly can confirm our recommended checklist for audit artifacts.

Best,
Tessa Morgan
Sr. Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
T: +1 (650) 555-0133

Attachment: redwood_privatedeploy_checklist.xlsx (checklist stub)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@harbortech.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Oct 2026 08:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Ingress conduit options — short matrix and numbers

On Thu, Oct 29, 2026 at 09:12 AM Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@harbortech.com> wrote:
> Attaching a quick topology...

Thanks for the clear ask — I put together a short comparison (attached as conduit_comparison_table.csv) and pulled some numbers you can use for capacity planning. Highlights:

1) PrivateLink (Interface endpoint)
- Pros: Isolated, per-service access; good for control-plane and request/response patterns; simple to lock down via endpoint policies.
- Cons: ENI throughput limits per AZ; youll want 23 endpoints spread across AZs for durability.
- Throughput: Baseline single ENI ~1-5 Gbps but practical sustained single-connection bandwidth often <1 Gbps depending on instance types. Use multiple parallel connections.
- Ops: Low for standard app traffic, medium if you manage many endpoints or require cross-account endpoints.

2) VPC Peering / Transit Gateway
- Pros: Very low latency, high throughput, predictable L4 connectivity; works well for bulk data and streaming.
- Cons: Cross-account peering exposes route tables; less granular service-level isolation. Transit Gateway adds cost but simplifies multi-VPC scaling.
- Throughput: effectively limited by instance/network performance and TGW attachments; comfortably supports sustained 1 Gbps+ workloads.

3) Site-to-site VPN
- Pros: Works for on-prem and hybrid; encrypted tunnel over public internet or Direct Connect; lower up-front change to network topology.
- Cons: Higher latency and jitter; maintenance (rekeying, tunnel monitoring); not ideal for sub-15ms latency SLOs unless paired with Direct Connect.

Failover patterns we recommend:
- Active control-plane via PrivateLink with peering/TGW path available as a warm standby for heavy batch traffic.
- Health checks and route preference: endpoint-client side health check -> endpoint fails -> route traffic via TGW peering with rate-limiting backpressure.

Operational notes:
- Auditability: enable VPC Flow Logs + CloudTrail for endpoint creation; export logs to a dedicated S3 bucket (Customer-managed encryption recommended).
- KMS: if HarborTech requires exclusive HSM-based keys, we can design for a customer-managed CMK (you retain rotation policies); otherwise Redwood-managed KMS with role-based access and logging works for most customers.

Ive attached the CSV with numbers and a simple decision tree. Happy to walk through it on the call Tessa proposed.

Ben Carter
Principal Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com

Attachment: conduit_comparison_table.csv (numeric comparison)

- Compliance: HarborTech is mid-audit for SOC2; we will insist on customer-managed CMK for the pilot if possible.

Liam O'Connor

title: Unexpected embedding usage — test environment traffic caused overage
preview:
From: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: AsterCloud Billing <billing@astercloud.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Oct 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — please investigate

Hi Ava,

We just received the October invoice and there is a ~10x spike in embedding-related charges for the period Oct 24–28. Our engineering team confirms we didn’t ramp production traffic — only some internal test runs on the sandbox. We estimate ~12M tokens but the invoice shows ~120M. Can you investigate what was counted as billable tokens and share the raw usage slice for that window?

We need an explanation for procurement and to determine if this is a metering/config issue on our side or something in Redwood’s tokenization/aggregation. We’ve attached the billing alert screenshot.

Thanks,
Marta Lopez
Head of Platform Operations, AsterCloud Inc.
marta.lopez@astercloud.com
+1 415-555-0123

[attachment: billing-alert-oct28.png]

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Oct 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — please investigate

Hi Marta — thanks for the flag and the screenshot. I’m looping in Soojin (billing) and Karthik (infra/telemetry). We’ll pull the raw usage export and a sample of requests for the window you called out.

Quick heads-up on what we’ll look for: tokenization differences between chunked documents vs. your expected counting, duplicated requests from CI/test harnesses, and any streaming/chunk replays that could have inflated counts.

Can you confirm whether the sandbox keys were exposed to a shared CI runner around Oct 24 (we’ve seen similar patterns when a load-test job got scheduled accidentally)? If helpful, we’ll DOA a provisional credit while we finish the audit.

I attached a small sample request extractor so you can see the fields we’ll compare.

— Ava
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
[attachment: sample-requests-clip.jsonl]

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Oct 2026 08:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — initial telemetry export attached

Hi team — I pulled the initial usage export for AsterCloud for Oct 20–31 and attached the slice (usage-export-20261029.csv). Preliminary observations:

- There is a cluster of requests between Oct 24 02:10–04:20 UTC with unusually high embedding token counts.
- The request metadata shows the same API key used across ~8 different client IPs (likely a CI runner cycling through ephemeral addresses).
- Many of the large-count entries have long 'document' payloads that were chunked into ~2–4 chunks each on the server side and each chunk was counted toward the token tally.

Next steps: Karthik, can you pull a sample of the raw request bodies and the server-side chunking logs for that interval (we need to confirm whether client-side chunking vs server-side chunking is responsible)? Marcus, can you sanity-check whether any retry/backoff behavior or streaming reconnects caused repeated charging for the same logical request?

I’ve CC’d Marta for visibility. If this turns out to be an unintended CI ramp, we should propose a one-time invoice adjustment and provide guidance to avoid repeats.

Thanks,
Soojin Lee
Revenue Operations Manager, Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 07:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — root cause notes

Folks — I grabbed server logs for the Oct 24 02:00–05:00 UTC window and sampled 150 requests (attached sample-requests-clip.jsonl was expanded on our side). Key findings:

- AsterCloud’s sandbox key appears to have been used by a scheduled job that re-run a document ingestion pipeline with long multi-page PDFs. Those documents were chunked server-side into 1k–2k token segments for embedding. Each chunk triggers an embedding call and is counted independently.
- There were intermittent client retries: we see several 409/timeout-induced retries where the client retried after a short backoff, but because the pipeline did not use idempotency tokens for embed operations, retries resulted in duplicate embedding calls.
- Streaming reconnects: for a small percentage (~4%) of requests the websocket/stream connection dropped and the client resumed by re-sending the last chunk without idempotency. That produced duplicate chargeable calls.

Action items we recommend to AsterCloud:
1) Rotate sandbox keys and lock the CI runner to a narrow IP range or use key restrictions (we can provide a how-to).
2) Add a deduplication idempotency token for batching/ingest jobs so retries are tolerated without duplicate billable calls.
3) Use our recommended chunking heuristics (we'll send a short doc) to reduce number of chunks per document.

From Redwood side, I’m proposing a provisional credit of 75% of the spike delta while we finish the full audit (we applied the provisional note to draft credit note id CN-20261101-P). Marcus — can you confirm the credit calc and whether any tokenization edge-case should be adjusted because of server-side chunking behavior?

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden embedding usage — credit proposal and next steps

Thanks — I validated the sample and the proposed credit math. Notes:

- We recomputed billable tokens using our conservative model (counting prompt + chunked doc tokens, collapsing immediate retries within 30s as duplicates where logs indicate identical payloads). Using that method, the spike delta is ~98M tokens. A 75% provisional credit equals ~73.5M tokens credited (I converted tokens to your plan's unit price to build CN-20261101-P).
- I confirmed that streaming reconnects accounted for ~3.9% of duplicates — those are included in the credit calculation.
- Attached: provisional-credit-note-20261101.pdf (internal finance template). If AsterCloud agrees, we’ll post the credit to the Nov invoice schedule and issue a final audit summary within 7 business days.

Marta — once you sign off on the provisional credit we’ll:
1) Apply the credit as a negative line on your next invoice (visible in the console).
2) Deliver the audit CSV showing deduped requests and a short tech note with recommended idempotency headers and chunking limits.

If AsterCloud prefers a one-time refund instead of a credit, Finance can do that but it takes an extra 5–7 business days to process.

— Marcus Lin
Principal Product Reliability Engineer, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com

On Sun, Nov 01, 2026 at 07:21 AM Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com> wrote:
> Folks — I grabbed server logs for the Oct 24 02:00–05:00 UTC window and sampled 150 requests...

To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 07:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden 10x jump in embedding usage (Oct 24-28) — root cause notes

Folks — I grabbed server logs for the Oct 24 02:00–05:00 UTC window and sampled 150 requests (attached sample-requests-clip.jsonl was expanded on our side). Key findings:

- AsterCloud’s sandbox key appears to have been used by a scheduled job that re-run a document ingestion pipeline with long multi-page PDFs. Those documents were chunked server-side into 1k–2k token segments for embedding. Each chunk triggers an embedding call and is counted independently.
- There were intermittent client retries: we see several 409/timeout-induced retries where the client retried after a short backoff, but because the pipeline did not use idempotency tokens for embed operations, retries resulted in duplicate embedding calls.
- Streaming reconnects: for a small percentage (~4%) of requests the websocket/stream connection dropped and the client resumed by re-sending the last chunk without idempotency. That produced duplicate chargeable calls.

Action items we recommend to AsterCloud:
1) Rotate sandbox keys and lock the CI runner to a narrow IP range or use key restrictions (we can provide a how-to).
2) Add a deduplication idempotency token for batching/ingest jobs so retries are tolerated without duplicate billable calls.
3) Use our recommended chunking heuristics (we'll send a short doc) to reduce number of chunks per document.

From Redwood side, I’m proposing a provisional credit of 75% of the spike delta while we finish the full audit (we applied the provisional note to draft credit note id CN-20261101-P). Marcus — can you confirm the credit calc and whether any tokenization edge-case should be adjusted because of server-side chunking behavior?

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@astercloud.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden embedding usage — credit proposal and next steps

Thanks — I validated the sample and the proposed credit math. Notes:

- We recomputed billable tokens using our conservative model (counting prompt + chunked doc tokens, collapsing immediate retries within 30s as duplicates where logs indicate identical payloads). Using that method, the spike delta is ~98M tokens. A 75% provisional credit equals ~73.5M tokens credited (I converted tokens to your plan's unit price to build CN-20261101-P).
- I confirmed that streaming reconnects accounted for ~3.9% of duplicates — those are included in the credit calculation.
- Attached: provisional-credit-note-20261101.pdf (internal finance template). If AsterCloud agrees, we’ll post the credit to the Nov invoice schedule and issue a final audit summary within 7 business days.

Marta — once you sign off on the provisional credit we’ll:
1) Apply the credit as a negative line on your next invoice (visible in the console).
2) Deliver the audit CSV showing deduped requests and a short tech note with recommended idempotency headers and chunking limits.

If AsterCloud prefers a one-time refund instead of a credit, Finance can do that but it takes an extra 5–7 business days to process.

— Marcus Lin
Principal Product Reliability Engineer, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com

title: Request: async run SLO tolerance and idempotency plan
preview:
From: Maya Singh <maya@acme.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 28 Oct 2026 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Async runs - request for SLO tolerance and idempotency confirmation

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the Dedicated cluster looked promising. We're moving towards production ingestion for our nightly enrichment pipeline (\"Nightly Enrich V2\") and need to lock down operational commitments so our compliance and platform teams can sign off. Two things we need clarified:

1) Job completion window: our runs are variable but the target is to have all jobs completed within 24 hours of submission. Given spikes, can Redwood provide a formal tolerance? e.g., 99% within 12 hours, 100% within 48 hours, with defined crediting if missed.

2) Retries & idempotency: our controller retries failed tasks aggressively. We need guidance on idempotency tokens you require so retries are safe across your async queue and any automatic server-side retries. Specifically: guaranteed-at-most-once vs at-least-once behavior, and any dedupe window we should assume.

I've attached a workload_summary.csv with job sizes and a signed sla_req_form.pdf to help. Can you put together a short mitigation plan and note any constraints (capacity, reserved windows, or required request headers)?

Appreciate it — Maya
Maya Singh
Head of Platform Engineering, ACME Corp
maya@acme.ai

Attachments: workload_summary.csv (csv), sla_req_form.pdf (pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Singh <maya@acme.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 28 Oct 2026 11:07:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Async runs - request for SLO tolerance and idempotency confirmation

Maya — thanks for the detail and the CSV. We'll take this through both product and infra. Quick ack and next steps below: 

- We can propose a temporary SLO carveout for Nightly Enrich V2 while we finalize capacity sizing (Dedicated).
- To scope the carveout precisely we need: peak concurrent tasks, distribution of small/medium/large job lengths (the CSV helps), and whether you require hard retry guarantees or can tolerate replay within a dedupe window.

Marcus — can you draft a technical mitigation plan and recommend the idempotency token format we should require (header name + TTL)? Laura — can you start loop-in for contract language for a temporary tolerance and the process for credits if the window is missed?

Thanks,
Ben

Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
